text: The surgeon performs a laparoscopic type C-I radical hysterectomy, beginning with a left pelvic lymphadenectomy. Adhesiolysis is conducted to enhance exposure of the left pelvic area. The left round ligament is coagulated and cut, followed by a peritoneal incision extended cranially and caudally. The vesico-umbilical artery is grasped and pulled medially to expose the entrance of the paravesical space, which is dissected down to the pelvic floor. Dissection continues along the vesico-hypogastric fascia, reaching the levator ani muscle and proceeding caudally towards the bifurcation of the iliac artery. The internal iliac artery and vesico-hypogastric fascia define the medial limits of the dissection.

The surgeon encounters the ureter medial to the internal iliac artery. After dissecting the ventral and medial limits of the paravesical space, the dissection shifts laterally, detaching the external iliac vessels from the psoas muscle. The dissection proceeds over the fascia of the psoas muscle, detaching paravisceral fat from its medial and lateral borders, and separating it from the external iliac vessels and parietal branches of the internal iliac vessels. The genitofemoral nerve remains intact, while lymphatic and fatty tissue is removed around the circumference of the external iliac vessels.

The surgeon meticulously dissects the parietal branches of the internal iliac artery and vein in the prespinous region, addressing the variable anatomy. The right pelvic lymphadenectomy is performed, with the removal of right pelvic lymph nodes following a similar technique as previously described.

The surgeon develops the vesicovaginal space by coagulating and cutting the peritoneum of the vesicovaginal fold from the left to right round ligaments. The urinary bladder is grasped with atraumatic forceps and pulled upwards, allowing dissection from the cervix and proximal vagina up to the vesicle trigone. The dissection proceeds laterally, developing the Yabuki fort spaces, with the distal ureters becoming visible.

The surgeon develops the right Latsko space, carefully dissecting to avoid damage to the pelvic plexus. Lymphatics medial to the internal iliac artery are removed, and the right vesico-umbilical artery is isolated, dissected from the ventral parametrum, and the superficial vesicle artery is coagulated and cut. The uterine artery is coagulated and cut near the internal iliac artery, followed by the superficial uterine vein. The right Okabayashi space is developed extensively, with the mesoureter and hypogastric nerve fibers dissected from the dorsal parametrium. The right deep uterine vein is isolated, coagulated, and cut medial to the ureter, preserving the branches of the pelvic plexus.

The surgeon performs ureter unroofing and manages the ventral parametrium on the right side by coagulating and cutting the ureteral branch of the uterine artery, allowing safe entry into the ureteric canal. The superficial layer of the vesicle uterine ligament is cut near the bladder wall, facilitating the lateralization of the distal ureter and exposing the medial portion of the deep layer of the vesicouterine ligament. The bladder branch of the pelvic splanchnic nerve is visible. On the left side, the vesico-umbilical artery is detached from the ventral parametria, and the left superior vesicle artery is coagulated and cut. The left uterine artery and superficial uterine vein are also coagulated and cut. The left Okabayashi space is developed, pushing the mesoureter and hypogastric nerve laterally. The ureter is carefully dissected from surrounding tissue, ensuring the ureteral sheath remains intact to minimize devascularization, and is traced up to its entry into the urinary bladder.

The surgeon manages the dorsal parametria by detaching and lateralizing the hypogastric nerves, allowing for radical posterior parametrial resection. The resection begins at the rectosigmoid colon and progresses towards the vagina, utilizing the Harmonic ACE device. The embryological plane between the mesorectum and dorsal parametrium is identified, and the rectum is grasped with atraumatic forceps and pulled cranially to reveal the rectovaginal plane. The rectum is safely dissected off the dorsal vagina, enabling radical resection of the dorsal parametria close to the rectum.

The surgeon uses a harmonic ACE device to resect the paraculpia along with the medial vascular portion of the deep layer of the vesico-cervical ligament. A colpotomy is performed, excising the uterus along with the proximal vagina using a ceramic colpotomizer of the Clermont-Ferrand uterine manipulator, allowing for adjustable excision length.

The surgeon dissects the common iliac and subaortic lymph nodes, using the right and left ureters as lateral landmarks for the lymphadenectomy. The scope is repositioned from the standard umbilical port to the supraumbilical port for improved visualization. The peritoneal incision is extended cranially up to the superior mesenteric artery.

The surgeon uses a bursing needle to suspend the edges of the dissected peritoneum, fully exposing the area for lymphadenectomy. Dissection begins over the common iliac arteries, with careful isolation and sparing of the superior hypogastric plexus. Fatty and lymphatic tissue around the aortic bifurcation is dissected away, preserving the superior hypogastric plexus and proximal hypogastric nerves. The common iliac, proximal para-aortic, and paracaival lymph nodes are then dissected.

The surgeon closes the vaginal cuff using two figure-of-eight, 2-0 absorbable intracorporeal sutures. The final view shows the paravisceral space cleared of lymphatic and fatty tissue, revealing the obturator nerve, parietal branch of internal iliac vessels, ventral part of the sciatic nerve, intrapelvic ischial spine, and sacrospinous ligament. The resected proximal vagina, dorsal parametria, and ventrolateral parametria are visible in one block.
